descriptions.text: As a journalist, you made headlines back in 2006 with a groundbreaking story. Years later, an anonymous call tied to that very event leads you to an horror house. But this house may belong to the Antichrist believed to appear at the end of days. You’re chasing a story… but it might be your last.

In 2006, as a journalist, you broke a sensational story that made your name known across the media.

Years later, you receive a mysterious call from an anonymous person someone connected to that very event.

You still see this story as headline material, and you set off toward an abandoned, horror house in hopes of uncovering the truth.

But there’s one problem: this terrifying place is believed, according to many faiths, to be the house of the Antichrist said to appear near the end of days.

In Christian eschatology, Antichrist, or in broader eschatology, Anti-Messiah, refers to a kind of entity prophesied by the Bible to oppose Jesus Christ and falsely substitute himself as a savior in Christ's place before the Second Coming. The term Antichrist (including one plural form) is found four times in the New Testament, solely in the First and Second Epistle of John. Antichrist is announced as one "who denies the Father and the Son."
The similar term pseudokhristos or "false Christ" is also found in the Gospels. In Matthew (chapter 24) and Mark (chapter 13), Jesus alerts his disciples not to be deceived by the false prophets, who will claim themselves to be the Christ, performing "great signs and wonders". Three other images often associated with Antichrist are the "little horn" in Daniel's final vision, the "man of sin" in Paul the Apostle's Second Epistle to the Thessalonians, and the Beast of the Sea in the Book of Revelation.
In the New Testament, particularly in the Johannine epistles, the term does not refer to a single individual but rather to a category of people opposing Christ, often called deceivers or false teachers. Early Church Fathers like Irenaeus, Tertullia
